ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'milk tea' class (FOODON_03301099); no specific green milk tea subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers tea-based beverages with milk as the closest defensible category; no green-milk-tea-specific code exists in the published FoodEx2 hierarchy. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for green milk tea beverage identified in the published SNOMED CT hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup), equivalent to 250 g, as commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and kopitiam stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Green Milk Tea (No Topping) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Green Milk Tea (No Topping), cr_id D08010037, with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea beverages — analogous estimate based on sweetened tea with milk (GI ~55) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for green milk tea exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the closest analogous entry for sweetened tea with milk, which has a GI of approximately 55 due to the combination of sucrose and milk lactose. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (15.35 g from 250 g serving) / 100 = 8.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Added sugar (6.1 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage independently raises blood pressure risk; sodium is low at 12 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid sweetened milk tea; request an unsweetened version or substitute with plain green tea, as the AHA (2018) identifies sugar-sweetened beverages as an independent risk factor for elevated blood pressure regardless of sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar (6.1 g/100 g), zero dietary fibre, and sugar-sweetened beverage classification cause rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely in its sweetened form; per ADA guidelines, any sugar-sweetened beverage is unsuitable for diabetes management, and only a zero-added-sugar version of green milk tea would be acceptable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Added sugar (6.1 g/100 g) and saturated fat from milk or creamer contribute to cardiovascular risk; low sodium (12 mg/100 g) is a positive factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid sweetened milk tea due to added sugar and saturated fat content; unsweetened green tea with low-fat milk is a preferable alternative that preserves the potential cardioprotective polyphenol benefit of green tea.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (6.1 g/100 g) with zero dietary fibre and no meaningful anti-inflammatory micronutrients negates the potential anti-inflammatory benefit of green tea polyphenols
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request an unsweetened version with low-fat dairy milk to preserve any anti-inflammatory benefit from green tea catechins, as added sugar in the sweetened form promotes pro-inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Milk Tea (No Topping) is a freshly prepared Singaporean speciality tea beverage made by combining brewed green tea with milk or non-dairy creamer and sugar, served without any additional toppings such as pearls, jelly, or pudding. It is widely available at bubble tea shops, kopitiams, and hawker stalls across Singapore. The beverage has a mild, slightly grassy green tea flavour balanced by the creaminess of milk or creamer and the sweetness of added sugar. Its texture is smooth and liquid, with no solid components in the no-topping variant. Per 100 g, it provides 46 kcal, 0.32 g protein, 2.41 g fat, and 6.14 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with negligible dietary fibre (0 g) and low sodium (12 mg). The saturated fat content is estimated at approximately 1.55 g/100 g from the milk or creamer component. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) due to its 6.1 g/100 g added sugar content and zero fibre, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease in its standard sweetened form. An unsweetened version with low-fat milk would substantially improve its health profile and preserve the potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant benefits of green tea catechins (EGCG). The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on analogous sweetened tea with milk data from the University of Sydney GI Database, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 8.4 per 250 ml serving.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010037) with a stated confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean food records. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: 1. Saturated fat estimated at 1.55 g/100 g based on typical milk/creamer composition for this beverage type; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item and are not available from other approved sources for this specific preparation. 3. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per the HPB source — this is a liquid beverage with no fibre-containing solid components. 4. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sweetened tea with milk from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for green milk tea exists in the approved GI source. 5. The food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per standard public health definitions (sugar ≥5 g/100 g in a beverage). 6. Halal status assumed based on standard ingredients (green tea, milk, sugar); no pork-derived or alcohol-containing ingredients identified. 7. Calcium reported as 2.0 mg/100 g per HPB source, which is notably low for a milk-containing beverage and may reflect a dilute preparation or creamer-based formulation.

Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' as GI 55 falls into the low GI category (GI <= 55).
Added 'Teh Susu Hijau (Tanpa Topping)' to the flat 'aliases' array to ensure consistency with 'aliases_by_language' as per q8 gate.
